---
title: "09 ¬∑ Optimal Policies and Optimal Values"
subtitle: "Define optimality; existence of an optimal policy and uniqueness of the optimal value."
lesson-id: "mfrl.optimal-policies"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.action-values"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "drafted"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest. Are you ready for this lesson?

1. From [Lesson 5](../05-state-values/index.qmd), can you compute
   $v_\pi(s)$ for a fixed policy on a small MDP?
2. From [Lesson 8](../08-action-values/index.qmd), can you write
   $q_\pi(s, a)$ and use it to identify the greedy action?
3. Can you compare two functions point-wise: "$f \le g$ means $f(x) \le g(x)$
   for every $x$"?

If a "no" appears, briefly revisit lessons 5 and 8.
:::

## Touch

You have two policies in mind for the same grid world. Call them $\pi_A$
and $\pi_B$. You can compute $v_{\pi_A}(s)$ and $v_{\pi_B}(s)$ for every
cell ‚Äî two side-by-side colour maps.

When is one policy *better* than the other? You might say "$\pi_A$ is
better than $\pi_B$ if $v_{\pi_A}(s) \ge v_{\pi_B}(s)$ for *every* state
$s$." That's strict ‚Äî both colour maps must be at least as bright on
$\pi_A$'s side as on $\pi_B$'s.

But what if $\pi_A$'s map is brighter in the top-left corner and $\pi_B$'s
is brighter in the bottom-right? Then neither is "better than" the other.
Does an optimal policy even exist?

```{ojs}
viewof scenario = Inputs.radio(
  ["A dominates", "Neither dominates", "Tied everywhere"],
  {value: "Neither dominates", label: "Compare two policies"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let A, B;
  if (scenario.startsWith("A")) { A = [3, 4, 5]; B = [2, 3, 4]; }
  else if (scenario.startsWith("Neither")) { A = [3, 4, 1]; B = [2, 3, 5]; }
  else { A = [3, 4, 5]; B = [3, 4, 5]; }
  return html`<pre style="font-size:0.95rem;">
state    v(œÄA)   v(œÄB)   œÄA ‚â• œÄB at this state?
1        ${A[0]}      ${B[0]}      ${A[0] >= B[0] ? "yes" : "no"}
2        ${A[1]}      ${B[1]}      ${A[1] >= B[1] ? "yes" : "no"}
3        ${A[2]}      ${B[2]}      ${A[2] >= B[2] ? "yes" : "no"}

œÄA ‚âΩ œÄB (everywhere)?  ${(A[0]>=B[0] && A[1]>=B[1] && A[2]>=B[2]) ? "YES" : "NO"}
</pre>`;
}
```

Caption: three states, two policies. The relation "$\pi_A \succeq \pi_B$"
demands $v_{\pi_A}(s) \ge v_{\pi_B}(s)$ at *every* state, not just on
average.

## Wonder

1. Could two policies tie on every state's value? Could one be tied at
   some states and strictly better at others?
2. If two policies don't dominate each other, which one would you call
   "optimal"?
3. Bellman's optimality theorem says an optimal $\pi^*$ *always exists*
   for finite MDPs. Why is that not obvious?

## Discover

### Problem 1 ‚Äî Define a partial order on policies

Define $\pi^* \succeq \pi$ to mean $v_{\pi^*}(s) \ge v_\pi(s)$ for *every*
$s \in S$. Show this is reflexive ($\pi \succeq \pi$) and transitive
(if $\pi_1 \succeq \pi_2$ and $\pi_2 \succeq \pi_3$ then $\pi_1 \succeq \pi_3$).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Reflexive:** $v_\pi(s) \ge v_\pi(s)$ for every $s$, trivially.

**Transitive:** $v_{\pi_1}(s) \ge v_{\pi_2}(s) \ge v_{\pi_3}(s)$ for
every $s$ chains to $v_{\pi_1}(s) \ge v_{\pi_3}(s)$.

But $\succeq$ is *not* total: there exist policies that don't satisfy
$\succeq$ in either direction. (Problem 2.)
</details>

### Problem 2 ‚Äî Two policies that don't compare

Construct a 2-state, 2-action MDP and two deterministic policies
$\pi_1, \pi_2$ such that neither $\pi_1 \succeq \pi_2$ nor $\pi_2 \succeq \pi_1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Make the actions specialise to different states.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$S = \{X, Y\}$, $A = \{a, b\}$. Self-loops: $P(X \mid X, \cdot) = 1$,
$P(Y \mid Y, \cdot) = 1$. (Each state is absorbing.) Rewards:
$R(X, a, X) = 1, R(X, b, X) = 0, R(Y, a, Y) = 0, R(Y, b, Y) = 1$.
$\gamma = 0.9$.

$\pi_1(X) = a, \pi_1(Y) = a$: $v_{\pi_1}(X) = 10$, $v_{\pi_1}(Y) = 0$.
$\pi_2(X) = b, \pi_2(Y) = b$: $v_{\pi_2}(X) = 0$, $v_{\pi_2}(Y) = 10$.

$\pi_1$ wins at $X$, $\pi_2$ wins at $Y$. Neither dominates.

But the policy $\pi_3(X) = a, \pi_3(Y) = b$ dominates *both*:
$v_{\pi_3}(X) = 10, v_{\pi_3}(Y) = 10$.
</details>

### Problem 3 ‚Äî Bellman's optimality theorem (statement)

State the following theorem. (You will *use* it in Problem 4 ‚Äî proof comes
in lessons 11‚Äì12.)

> **Theorem (Bellman).** For a finite MDP with $\gamma \in [0, 1)$, there
> exists at least one policy $\pi^*$ such that $\pi^* \succeq \pi$ for
> *every* policy $\pi$. Moreover, $\pi^*$ can be chosen *deterministic*
> and *stationary*.

Why is this surprising?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Surprising because the partial order $\succeq$ is *not* total ‚Äî most
pairs of policies don't compare. Yet the theorem says one policy beats
*everyone* simultaneously: a global maximum in a partial order. That is
not obvious.

The proof uses the contraction property of the Bellman optimality
operator (lessons 11‚Äì12) and the existence of a fixed point. Bellman
proved it in 1957.
</details>

### Problem 4 ‚Äî Define $v^*$

Let $\pi^*$ be a policy from Problem 3. Define
$$v^*(s) = v_{\pi^*}(s) = \max_\pi v_\pi(s).$$
Show that the right-hand "$\max$" is well-defined: the maximum is achieved
*by the same $\pi^*$ at every state*.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Suppose $\pi^* \succeq \pi$ for all $\pi$. Then for each $s$:
$v_{\pi^*}(s) \ge v_\pi(s)$ for every $\pi$, so $v_{\pi^*}(s) = \sup_\pi v_\pi(s) = \max_\pi v_\pi(s)$
(supremum is attained by $\pi^*$).

Equivalently, the per-state maxima are *all attained by the same $\pi^*$* ‚Äî
a non-trivial claim, since you might worry that no single policy attains
the maximum everywhere. The theorem says: it does.
</details>

### Problem 5 ‚Äî Optimal policies are not unique

Construct a small MDP with two distinct optimal policies. Use it to argue
that *while $v^*$ is unique, $\pi^*$ may not be*.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Make two actions tie in $q^*$ at some state.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$S = \{X\}$, $A = \{a, b\}$. Both actions self-loop with reward 1.
$v_\pi(X) = 1/(1 - \gamma) = 10$ for *any* $\pi$, including
$\pi(a) = 1$, $\pi(b) = 1$, or $\pi(a) = 0.5$. **Many optimal policies,
one optimal value.**

Generally, ties in the greedy step ($\arg\max_a q^*$) produce multiple
optimal policies. The optimal *value* $v^*$ is always unique (it solves
the BOE ‚Äî lesson 10).
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
$v^*$ is **unique** but $\pi^*$ is generally **not**. Different
$\pi^*$'s arise from ties in the optimal action at some state. This
matters when comparing trained agents ‚Äî two agents with identical $v^*$
may take quite different actions in the wild, all of them optimal.
Practical RL almost always tiebreaks deterministically (smallest index,
etc.) so reproducible behaviour is recovered.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **[optimal state value](/glossary.qmd#optimal-state-value)** is
$$v^*(s) = \max_\pi v_\pi(s) \quad \forall s \in S.$$
A policy $\pi^*$ is **[optimal](/glossary.qmd#optimal-policy)** if
$v_{\pi^*}(s) = v^*(s)$ for every $s$. For a finite MDP with $\gamma < 1$,
an optimal *deterministic stationary* policy exists; $v^*$ is unique but
$\pi^*$ may not be.

:::

A verse to remember it by:

> *Wins at every state ‚Äî the gold standard.*
> *Such a $\pi^*$ exists, says Bellman.*
> *The value is one, the policy may split,*
> *but every $\pi^*$ scores the same hit.*

## Apply

### Worked example ‚Äî enumerate policies on a 2-state MDP

Take the 2-state MDP from Discover P2 (separate self-loops, rewards
arranged so $a$ likes $X$, $b$ likes $Y$). Four deterministic policies:

| Policy | $\pi(X)$ | $\pi(Y)$ | $v(X)$ | $v(Y)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\pi_1$ | $a$ | $a$ | 10 | 0 |
| $\pi_2$ | $a$ | $b$ | 10 | 10 |
| $\pi_3$ | $b$ | $a$ | 0 | 0 |
| $\pi_4$ | $b$ | $b$ | 0 | 10 |

**Step 1 ‚Äî find $v^*$.** Per-state maximum: $v^*(X) = \max(10, 10, 0, 0) = 10$.
$v^*(Y) = \max(0, 10, 0, 10) = 10$. So $v^*(X) = v^*(Y) = 10$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî find $\pi^*$.** A policy attains $v^*$ at every state iff
$v_\pi(X) = 10$ *and* $v_\pi(Y) = 10$. Looking at the table: only $\pi_2$
qualifies. **$\pi^*$ is unique here:** $\pi^*(X) = a, \pi^*(Y) = b$.

**Step 3 ‚Äî verify Bellman's theorem.** $\pi_2 \succeq \pi_1, \pi_3, \pi_4$
(check each: $\pi_2$ ties or beats them at every state). The optimal
policy exists and is found by inspection.

### Practice ‚Äî Warm

**W1.** In the worked example, is $\pi_1 \succeq \pi_3$? $\pi_3 \succeq \pi_4$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi_1$ vs $\pi_3$: $v_{\pi_1}(X) = 10 \ge 0 = v_{\pi_3}(X)$, and
$v_{\pi_1}(Y) = 0 = v_{\pi_3}(Y)$. So $\pi_1 \succeq \pi_3$.

$\pi_3$ vs $\pi_4$: $v_{\pi_3}(X) = 0 = v_{\pi_4}(X)$, but
$v_{\pi_3}(Y) = 0 < 10 = v_{\pi_4}(Y)$. So $\pi_4 \succeq \pi_3$ (not
$\pi_3 \succeq \pi_4$).
</details>

**W2.** For a 1-state MDP with all actions giving $R = 5$, $\gamma = 0.9$,
how many optimal policies are there?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Infinitely many ‚Äî every policy has $v_\pi(X) = 50$. The optimal value is
unique ($v^*(X) = 50$), the optimal policy is wide open.
</details>

**W3.** Given $v^*$ and the model $(P, R, \gamma)$, write a formula for
the greedy action at $s$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$a^*(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) [R(s, a, s') + \gamma v^*(s')]$.

This is the *one-step look-ahead* greedy rule. Lesson 10 will show it
defines an optimal policy.
</details>

### Practice ‚Äî Working

**P1.** A 2-state MDP $\{X, Y\}$ with two actions $\{a, b\}$. Transitions
deterministic: $a$ at any state stays put; $b$ at any state swaps to the
other. Rewards: $R(X, \cdot, X) = 0$, $R(X, b, Y) = 0$, $R(Y, \cdot, Y) = 1$,
$R(Y, b, X) = 0$. $\gamma = 0.9$. Enumerate all 4 deterministic policies
and find $\pi^*, v^*$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Compute $v_\pi$ for each of the four policies.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- $\pi_1$: $a, a$. Both states absorbing. $v(X) = 0, v(Y) = 1/(1-0.9) = 10$.
- $\pi_2$: $a, b$. $X$ absorbs, $Y$ swaps to $X$. From $Y$ the trajectory
  is $Y \to X \to X \to \ldots$, rewards $1, 0, 0, \ldots$, $v(Y) = 1$.
  $v(X) = 0$.
- $\pi_3$: $b, a$. $X$ swaps to $Y$, $Y$ absorbs. From $X$: $X \to Y \to Y \to \ldots$,
  rewards $0, 1, 1, \ldots$, $v(X) = \gamma/(1-\gamma) = 9$. $v(Y) = 10$.
- $\pi_4$: $b, b$. Both swap. From $X$: $X \to Y \to X \to Y \ldots$,
  rewards $0, 1, 0, 1, \ldots$, $v(X) = \gamma/(1 - \gamma^2) = 0.9/0.19 \approx 4.737$.
  From $Y$: $Y \to X \to Y \to X$, rewards $0, 0, 1, 0, 1, \ldots$ ‚Äî wait,
  $R(Y, b, X) = 0$, then $X \to Y$ gives $R(X, b, Y) = 0$, then $Y \to X$
  gives $R(Y, b, X) = 0$. Rewards stay 0! Recompute. $v(Y) = 0, v(X) = 0$.

Per-state max: $v^*(X) = 9$ (achieved by $\pi_3$), $v^*(Y) = 10$
(achieved by $\pi_1$ and $\pi_3$). Need a single policy hitting both:
**$\pi_3$**. So $\pi^* = \pi_3$, $v^* = (9, 10)^T$. Unique optimal here.
</details>

**P2.** *Tied actions.* Modify P1 so that the optimal $\pi^*$ is *not*
unique. Sketch how.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Add a third action $c$ that behaves identically to $b$ at $X$
($P(Y \mid X, c) = 1$, $R(X, c, Y) = 0$). Then $\pi(X) = b$ and
$\pi(X) = c$ both yield identical futures. Two distinct optimal policies,
same $v^*$.
</details>

**P3.** Show that if $v_{\pi_1}(s_0) > v_{\pi_2}(s_0)$ for some specific
$s_0$, then **$\pi_2 \not\succeq \pi_1$**. (The relation $\succeq$ is
sensitive to *every* state.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi_2 \succeq \pi_1$ would require $v_{\pi_2}(s) \ge v_{\pi_1}(s)$ for
every $s$, including $s_0$. But $v_{\pi_1}(s_0) > v_{\pi_2}(s_0)$
contradicts that. So $\pi_2 \not\succeq \pi_1$.

Note this does *not* say $\pi_1 \succeq \pi_2$ ‚Äî neither may dominate.
</details>

### Practice ‚Äî Stretch

**S1.** Argue that "comparing average values" is **not** the same as the
$\succeq$ relation. Give a small MDP where one policy wins on average but
loses on $\succeq$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Re-use the 2-state MDP from Discover P2.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi_1$ from Discover P2 has $v(X) = 10, v(Y) = 0$, mean 5.
$\pi_2$ has $v(X) = 0, v(Y) = 10$, mean 5. Equal averages, but neither
$\succeq$ the other.

Conversely, if you weight states by some distribution $\rho$, you can
order policies *totally* by $\sum_s \rho(s) v_\pi(s)$ ‚Äî this is the
*objective* used in policy-gradient methods (lessons 33‚Äì37). But the BOE
(lesson 10) targets the stronger $\succeq$ ordering.
</details>

**S2.** *Stationary deterministic suffices.* Sketch why, in a finite MDP
with $\gamma < 1$, no time-varying or stochastic policy can do strictly
better than the best stationary deterministic one.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Use the BOE (lesson 10): the optimal $q^*$ is well-defined, and at every
state there's an action achieving the max.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The BOE (lesson 10) gives $v^*(s) = \max_a [R + \gamma \mathbb{E}[v^*(s')]]$
‚Äî a *deterministic* maximisation. The $\arg\max$ is a deterministic
action; choosing it at every visit to $s$ defines a deterministic
stationary policy that achieves $v^*$. So *no policy can do better than
$v^*$, and a deterministic stationary one always achieves $v^*$.*
Time-varying or stochastic policies might tie but cannot beat.

This is why the entire RL literature defaults to "find the best
deterministic policy" without apology: the maths says nothing else is
needed.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The existence of an optimal *deterministic* policy for finite MDPs is
nontrivial ‚Äî Bellman's 1957 *Dynamic Programming* established it as a
theorem, not a definition. He proved the Bellman optimality equation has
a fixed point and that the policy "act greedily with respect to the
fixed point" is optimal. The argument is reproduced essentially verbatim
in modern textbooks (Puterman, Sutton & Barto). The non-obvious step is
that the *same* $\pi^*$ wins at every state simultaneously ‚Äî Bellman's
genuine theorem, not a triviality.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Optimal means highest average value."** No ‚Äî optimal in the RL
   sense means $v_{\pi^*}(s) \ge v_\pi(s)$ at *every* state. Average
   value is a weaker objective.
2. **"$\pi^*$ is unique."** $v^*$ is unique; $\pi^*$ generally is not.
   Ties in the greedy step produce multiple optimal policies.
3. **"Stochastic policies might be optimal in some MDPs."** In *finite*
   MDPs with $\gamma < 1$, a deterministic stationary $\pi^*$ always
   exists. Stochastic optimality arises in two-player or partially
   observable settings, not in vanilla MDPs.
:::

### Takeaways

1. The partial order $\pi^* \succeq \pi$ requires
   $v_{\pi^*}(s) \ge v_\pi(s)$ at *every* state ‚Äî strict
   *Pareto-domination* on the value function.
2. The [optimal state value](/glossary.qmd#optimal-state-value)
   $v^*(s) = \max_\pi v_\pi(s)$ is unique. The
   [optimal policy](/glossary.qmd#optimal-policy) $\pi^*$ achieving $v^*$
   exists (Bellman's theorem) but may not be unique.
3. For finite MDPs, $\pi^*$ can always be chosen *stationary* and
   *deterministic*. Time-variation and randomness are not needed.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you, on a blank sheet, define $\pi^* \succeq \pi$ and explain
   why it is a partial (not total) order?
2. Did you finish W1‚ÄìS1 without peeking past *Hint 1*?
3. Can you state in one sentence why $v^*$ is unique but $\pi^*$ may
   not be?

- **3 yes:** continue to lesson 10 (the BOE).
- **2 yes:** redo P1 ‚Äî the enumeration is the cleanest concrete grasp
  of the optimality theorem.
- **0‚Äì1 yes:** re-read *Discover*. Problems 2 and 3 together capture
  the partial-order subtlety and the existence theorem.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **`mfrl.action-values`** ‚Äî for the greedy action $a^* = \arg\max_a q$.

### What this unlocks

- **Lesson 10 ‚Äî BOE.** The Bellman optimality equation defines
  $v^*$ recursively.
- **Lesson 12 ‚Äî Uniqueness of $v^*$.** A consequence of the BOE +
  contraction argument.
- **Lesson 14 ‚Äî Policy iteration.** Iteratively improves toward $\pi^*$.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Every RL algorithm targets either $v^*$ or $q^*$ ‚Äî they are
the goalposts. AlphaZero approximates $v^*$ for board games; DQN
approximates $q^*$ for Atari; PPO climbs toward $\pi^*$ via gradients.
The framework of "optimal value, optimal policy" is what makes "RL" a
single subject rather than a grab bag of algorithms.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research / control.** Optimal control problems (LQR, HJB equation in
continuous time) are the continuous-state analogue: an optimal
policy exists, the optimal value is unique. The discrete and continuous
worlds use the same vocabulary.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view; "$v^*$ is unique, $\pi^*$ exists" is
the headline.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), Chapter 3 ¬ß1-2. Cite freely; never paste Zhao's prose.
- Touchstones honoured: Russian school (Discover *constructs* the
  examples that motivate the theorem), Better Explained ("$v^*$ unique,
  $\pi^*$ may not be" is the Aha!).
:::
